title: PACE: Power-Aware Compliance via Hardware Energy Tanks
abstract: Admittance control enables safe physical human-robot interaction (pHRI), but on battery-powered manipulators it creates a hidden crisis: conventional stability proofs guarantee passivity in theory yet permit instantaneous power bursts exceeding 200 W that drain batteries nonlinearly, trip thermal-protection circuits, and collapse joint velocity loops under voltage sag. We present PACE (Power-Aware Compliance Engine), a certifiably-stable admittance controller that co-designs interaction compliance with a real-time power budget. PACE introduces a hardware energy tank, a Lyapunov-certified scalar state integrated on FPGA at 50 kHz, that continuously modulates the admittance stiffness and damping matrices to keep instantaneous draw below a user-specified budget $P_{\max }$ while preserving interaction quality. We prove PACE renders the closed-loop system passive with respect to a modified storage function even when the budget saturates, and we establish a formal sampled-data stability result showing that Euler integration of the tank state at sampling period $T_{s}$ preserves the passivity certificate provided $T_{s} \leq \lambda _{\min }(\mathbf {D}_{d}^{\min }) / (2L_{K} L_{v} + \gamma L_{P})$, where $L_{K}$, $L_{v}$, and $L_{P}$ bound the compliance rate, peak end-effector velocity, and power Lipschitz constant respectively; at 50 kHz ($T_{s}\!=\!20\,\mu$s) this condition is satisfied with a margin of $4.1\,\times$. Experiments on a battery-powered 7-DOF Kinova Gen3 arm demon

title: Surface-Conforming Modular Jamming Pads for Enhanced Contact and Friction
abstract: Granular jamming has been widely explored for adaptive robotic grasping, but conventional designs typically rely on single spherical jamming structures that limit contact distribution and modularity. This paper presents a flexure-based robotic gripper integrating modular square-shaped granular jamming pads as distributed adaptive contact interfaces. The design combines structural compliance from paired-Crossed Flexural Hinges (p-CFH) with material compliance from granular jamming, enabling passive surface conformity while improving frictional coupling and pad maintainability. Systematic experiments analyze the influence of pad geometry, granular material, gap distance, pad height, and vacuum pressure on grasp performance. Results show that the proposed square pad achieves 280% higher grip force than a spherical pad, while providing stable jamming behavior with EPDM granules. Compared with silicone pads, the jamming pad produces 30% higher friction and 197% larger contact area under identical loading conditions. Additional experiments further demonstrate robust grasping under varying contact orientations, positional offsets, and fragile objects. These results demonstrate that modular granular-jamming pads can serve as effective distributed contact interfaces for multi-finger robotic grippers.

title: Learning Visually Interpretable Oscillator Networks for Soft Continuum Robots From Video
abstract: Learning soft continuum robot (SCR) dynamics from video offers flexibility but existing methods lack interpretability or rely on prior assumptions. Model-based approaches require prior knowledge and manual design. We bridge this gap by introducing: (1) The Attention Broadcast Decoder (ABCD), a plug-and-play module for autoencoder-based latent dynamics learning that generates pixel-accurate attention maps localizing each latent dimension’s contribution while filtering static backgrounds, enabling visual interpretability via spatially grounded latents and on-image overlays. (2) Visual Oscillator Networks (VONs), a 2D latent oscillator network coupled to ABCD attention maps for on-image visualization of learned masses, coupling stiffness, and forces, thereby enabling mechanical interpretability. We validate our approach on single- and double-segment SCRs, demonstrating that ABCD-based models significantly improve multi-step prediction accuracy with 5.8× error reduction for Koopman operators and 3.5× for oscillator networks on a two-segment robot. VONs autonomously discover a chain structure of oscillators. This fully data-driven approach yields compact, mechanically interpretable models with potential relevance for future control applications.

title: SwarmForage: Emergent Division of Labor in 1000-Robot Swarms
